questions.prompt: What does Auto Layout solve that constraints don't?

choices.text: Parent responds to children — frame sizes based on content; spacing distributes automatically; reflow on add/remove. Constraints make CHILDREN respond to parent — when parent resizes, children resize per their constraints. Two different directions of dependency. For a button hugging its text: parent depends on child = Auto Layout. For a header sticking to top of screen: child depends on parent = constraints. For a list where adding items pushes others down: Auto Layout. For a sticky footer pinned to bottom: constraints. Modern Figma work is mostly Auto Layout (production interfaces are content-driven); constraints fill gaps for fixed-position decoration. Auto Layout = Figma's Flexbox equivalent; constraints = Figma's absolute-positioning. Most production designs use both: Auto Layout for structured content + constraints for floating overlays / decorative elements within Auto Layout frames.

questions.explanation: Parent responds to children + reflows automatically. Constraints make children respond to parent.

questions.prompt: Nav: logo left, user menu right, expanding gap between. Auto Layout setup?

choices.text: Horizontal Auto Layout with 'Space between' distribution. Set AL spacing to 'auto' (click the space-between icon) — Figma distributes children to fill parent width with logo at start, user menu at end, gap absorbing middle. As parent resizes, gap expands/contracts; logo and menu stay anchored to edges. Alternative: insert a spacer child (empty AL frame, fill width) between logo and menu — same effect manually. 'Space between' is cleaner; spacer is more explicit. For three-region layouts (left + center + right), two spacers around center, or just rely on 'space between' if you only have two regions of content. Most nav bars are two-region; space-between is the right pattern. Resize the nav by changing parent width to verify; logo and menu stay anchored while center expands. Standard pattern across UI tools (CSS flexbox justify-content: space-between equivalent).

questions.explanation: Horizontal AL with 'Space between' distribution. Middle gap absorbs space; ends anchored.

questions.prompt: Button width in Auto Layout: best setting?

choices.text: Hug. Button width should follow text content — short label = narrow; long label = wide — with consistent padding (typically 16px horizontal). 'Hug' sets frame width = sum of children widths + padding; change text, button auto-resizes. Counterpart: height also typically 'hug.' 'Fixed' would specify a width that doesn't adapt — short labels get whitespace, long labels overflow/truncate, both look broken. 'Fill' would stretch to parent width — appropriate for FULL-WIDTH CTAs (button at bottom of mobile screen) but wrong for inline buttons. Standard button: hug × hug. Full-width CTA: fill × hug. Icon-only button: fixed × fixed (e.g., 44×44 for touch target). Variants: hug with min-width (button stays at least 88pt even for 'OK') using Auto Layout's min constraint.

questions.explanation: Hug — button width tracks text length + padding. Fill is for full-width CTAs; fixed loses adaptability.

questions.prompt: Layer set to 'fill' inside Auto Layout but doesn't expand. Most likely cause?

choices.text: Parent frame is set to 'hug.' Fill only works when parent has DEFINED size — when parent is fixed or fill itself (so there's a known container size to fill). When parent is 'hug,' parent depends on children's content size; a child set to 'fill' inside a hugging parent has nothing to fill (parent is sized by content, and child's content drives that size). The mutual dependency resolves by ignoring fill; layer takes content size as if 'hug.' Fix: change parent to fill (if parent's parent has space) or fixed (specify explicit size); now child can fill it. Common debugging step: walk up parent chain checking sizing modes; first 'hug' ancestor breaks fill below. The mental model: 'fill needs a container with known size to fill.' For full-width content areas: ensure the chain from screen frame down has fill or fixed at each level so fill propagates. Most common Auto Layout confusion; recognizing it solves most layout debugging.

questions.explanation: Parent set to 'hug.' Fill needs fixed/fill parent. Walk up; first hug ancestor breaks fill below.

questions.prompt: Best approach to building complex nested Auto Layout screens?

choices.text: Bottom-up. Build smallest reusable components first (row, button, avatar), verify each resizes correctly in isolation, then compose into sections, then sections into screens. Top-down (start with screen frame, fill in details) typically requires rework when you realize the section layout doesn't accommodate inner pieces; bottom-up means each layer is solid before the next builds on it. When you assemble a section from already-working components, the section composes correctly. Common pattern for production designs: build atomic components first (often into a Components page or library), then arrange them in screen layouts. This matches engineering — small components composed up. Bonus: bottom-up components become library candidates; less rework as design system emerges. Top-down produces ad-hoc layouts that don't reuse well.

questions.explanation: Bottom-up — smallest reusable components first → sections → screens. Matches engineering composition.
